frameworks.name: sentiment prediction error
frameworks.one_line: Difference between expected and actual emotional tone drives post-conversation happiness.

frameworks.name: computational modeling of dialogue affect
frameworks.one_line: Trial-by-trial sentiment mismatch history is modeled to predict subjective well-being.

frameworks.name: expressive writing / journaling
frameworks.one_line: Journaling on matched topics serves as the control condition.

core_question: How do conversations with an AI chatbot influence subjective momentary happiness compared to journaling?
key_assumptions: Momentary happiness is comparable across chat and journaling conditions on matched topics
LLM-based sentiment analysis yields valid affect estimates over a dialogue
Expectations about conversational emotional tone shape subsequent affect
manipulations: Modality: AI chatbot conversation vs. written journal entry
Topic valence (e.g., negative topics like depression or guilt vs. other randomly assigned topics)
key_findings: Post-conversation happiness was higher after AI chat than after journaling
The benefit was strongest for negative topics
The chatbot mirrored user sentiment while maintaining a positivity bias
On negative topics, participants' sentiment drifted toward the AI's positivity
interpretations: History of sentiment prediction errors during dialogue predicts greater post-conversation happiness, implicating emotional expectations as a central mechanism
